mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive survey of mathematical history from Babylonian algebra and Egyptian mathematics (A.1.a.i), through Greek axiomatic proof (A.1.a.ii), Islamic algebra and transmission (A.1.a.iii), to calculus by Newton and Leibniz (A.1.b.i), 18th-century analysis (A.1.b.ii), and 19th-20th century developments in non-Euclidean geometry, set theory, and group theory (A.1.b.iii).

Covers historical development from ancient geometry/arithmetic through the foundational crisis and axiomatic method (A.1, B), including all major branches whose historical evolution is traced in A.2, and foundations of mathematics (B).

Historical development of algebra from Babylonian and Greek contributions (A.2.e.i), through Islamic formalism (A.2.e.ii), to 19th-century structural generalization including abstract algebra (A.2.e.iii).

Historical development of geometry from ancient land measurement (A.2.d.i), through analytic geometry (A.2.d.ii), non-Euclidean geometries (A.2.d.iv), to modern axiomatic methods and topology (A.2.d.vi); Euclidean axiomatic foundations (B.1.a) and non-Euclidean alternatives (B.1.b).

Historical development of numeral systems from Egyptian and Babylonian grouping systems (A.2.a.i) through positional notation (A.2.a.ii); foundational arithmetic and analysis via the genetic method (B.2.a).
ZFC axiomatic set theory and cardinality of infinite sets relate to cardinal number and theory of sets (B.2.b); foundational role connects to the crisis in foundations after 1900 (B.3).
Euler's contributions to analysis, number theory, and mathematical notation in the 18th century (A.1.b.ii); founding of graph theory and topology connects to modern geometrical ideas (A.2.d.vi).

First systematic treatise on algebra bridging Indian, Greek, and Islamic traditions represents Islamic mathematics and its transmission to the West (A.1.a.iii); introduced decimal positional number system (A.2.a.ii); contributions from the Orient, India, and the Islamic world in algebra (A.2.e.ii).
The Elements synthesized Greek mathematics into a deductive axiomatic system (A.1.a.ii); foundational role in geometry (A.2.d.i); the axiomatic method in Euclidean geometry (B.1, B.1.a).
Origins in Euler's polyhedron formula and formalization in the early 20th century represent 19th-20th century developments (A.1.b.iii); modern geometrical ideas including topology (A.2.d.vi).

Historical development of number concepts from natural numbers through complex and transfinite numbers relates to numeral systems (A.2.a); foundational construction of number systems via the genetic method (B.2.a); cardinality and set-theoretic foundations (B.2.b).

Incompleteness theorems set permanent limits on mathematical provability, central to the crisis in foundations after 1900 (B.3); results about paradoxes and consistency (B.3.a); contributions to Logicism, Formalism, and the metamathematical method (B.3.c).
Greek contributions to abstract number theory (A.1.a.ii); 19th-20th century developments including algebraic and analytic number theory (A.1.b.iii).
Independently formulated by Newton and Leibniz in the late 17th century, later rigourised through limits -- directly corresponds to the 17th-century development of calculus (A.1.b.i).

Anticipated integral calculus via the method of exhaustion and established foundational principles of statics, representing Greek and Hellenistic mathematical achievement (A.1.a.ii); geometry contributions (A.2.d.i).

Foundational results in number theory and non-Euclidean geometry represent 19th-century developments (A.1.b.iii); contributions to non-Euclidean geometry (A.2.d.iv); fundamental theorem of algebra and modern European algebra (A.2.e.iii).
19th-century development of group, ring, and field theory represents modern structural generalization (A.1.b.iii, A.2.e.iii).
Co-developed infinitesimal calculus with Leibniz in the 17th century, directly corresponding to the discovery of calculus (A.1.b.i).

Islamic Golden Age mathematics including algebra, geometry, trigonometry corresponds to Islamic mathematics and its transmission to the West (A.1.a.iii); development of positional numeral systems (A.2.a.ii); Islamic contributions to algebra (A.2.e.ii).
Egyptian practical mathematics and numeral systems (A.1.a.i, A.2.a.i); Egyptian geometry (A.2.d.i).
Babylonian mathematics including algebra, astronomy, and numeral systems originating in Mesopotamia (A.1.a.i); Babylonian numerals (A.2.a.i); Babylonian contributions to algebra (A.2.e.i).
Peano axioms and set-theoretic construction of natural numbers relate to arithmetic and analysis via the genetic method (B.2.a) and the concept of cardinal number (B.2.b).
18th-century advances in analysis by Euler and others (A.1.b.ii); 19th-century rigourisation and development of real, complex, and functional analysis (A.1.b.iii).
Developed in Hellenistic astronomy and further by Indian and Islamic scholars, corresponding to Islamic mathematical transmission (A.1.a.iii) and historical geometry (A.2.d.i).
Formalized in the 19th century via set theory, representing 19th-century mathematical development (A.1.b.iii); central to analysis and arithmetic foundations (B.2.a).
Greek axiomatic proof method (A.1.a.ii); the axiomatic method based on axioms and inference rules (B.1); the formal axiomatic method (B.1.c).

Non-Euclidean geometries by Gauss and Riemann demonstrated curved alternatives to Euclidean space (A.2.d.iv); philosophical aspects of geometry (A.2.d.v); non-Euclidean geometry in foundations (B.1.b).
Greek mathematics including philosophy, natural science, and axiomatic proof (A.1.a.ii); Greek geometry (A.2.d.i); Greek contributions to algebra (A.2.e.i).
Renaissance symbolic algebra and early modern scientific observation mark the beginning of the modern period in mathematics (A.1.b).
Evolution of digital devices from abacus to modern electronic digital computers in the history of calculatory science (A.2.c.iii).
The 17th-century Scientific Revolution encompassed the development of calculus and mathematization of nature (A.1.b.i).
